South African system of racial segregation

This article is about apartheid in South Africa. For apartheid as defined in international law, see [Crime of apartheid](/wiki/Crime_of_apartheid "Crime of apartheid"). For other uses, see [Apartheid (disambiguation)](/wiki/Apartheid_(disambiguation) "Apartheid (disambiguation)").

**Apartheid** ([/əˈpɑːrt(h)aɪt/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*ə-PART-(h)yte*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), especially [South African English](/wiki/South_African_English "South African English"): [/əˈpɑːrt(h)eɪt/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*ə-PART-(h)ayt*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans_language "Afrikaans language"): [[aˈpart(ɦ)əit]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Afrikaans "Help:IPA/Afrikaans") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q14196_(afr)-Oesjaar-apartheid.wav "File:LL-Q14196 (afr)-Oesjaar-apartheid.wav"); transl. "separateness", lit. 'aparthood') was a system of institutionalised [racial segregation](/wiki/Racial_segregation "Racial segregation") that existed in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") and [South West Africa](/wiki/South_West_Africa "South West Africa")[[a]](#cite_note-1) (now [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia "Namibia")) from 1948 to the early 1990s.[[note 1]](#cite_note-1990s-5) It was characterised by an [authoritarian](/wiki/Authoritarianism "Authoritarianism") political culture based on *[baasskap](/wiki/Baasskap "Baasskap")* (lit. 'boss-ship' or 'boss-hood'), which ensured that South Africa was dominated politically, socially, and economically by the nation's minority [white population](/wiki/White_South_Africans "White South Africans").[[4]](#cite_note-Mayne-6) Under this [minoritarian](/wiki/Minoritarianism "Minoritarianism") system, white citizens held the highest status, followed by [Indians](/wiki/Indian_South_Africans "Indian South Africans"), [Coloureds](/wiki/Coloureds "Coloureds") and [black Africans](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_South_Africa#Black_South_Africans "Ethnic groups in South Africa"), in that order.[[4]](#cite_note-Mayne-6) The economic legacy and social effects of apartheid continue to the present day, particularly [inequality](/wiki/Inequality_in_post-apartheid_South_Africa "Inequality in post-apartheid South Africa").[[5]](#cite_note-7)[[6]](#cite_note-8)[[7]](#cite_note-9)[[8]](#cite_note-10)

Broadly speaking, apartheid was delineated into *petty apartheid*, which entailed the segregation of public facilities and social events, and *grand apartheid*, which strictly segregated housing and [employment opportunities](/wiki/Segregation_in_employment_opportunities "Segregation in employment opportunities") by race.[[9]](#cite_note-Crompton-11) The first apartheid law was the [Prohibition of Mixed Marriages Act, 1949](/wiki/Prohibition_of_Mixed_Marriages_Act,_1949 "Prohibition of Mixed Marriages Act, 1949"), followed closely by the [Immorality Amendment Act](/wiki/Immorality_Act "Immorality Act") of 1950, which made it illegal for most South African citizens to [marry or pursue sexual relationships across racial lines](/wiki/Anti-miscegenation_laws "Anti-miscegenation laws").[[10]](#cite_note-Routledge-12) The [Population Registration Act, 1950](/wiki/Population_Registration_Act,_1950 "Population Registration Act, 1950") classified all South Africans into one of four racial groups based on appearance, known ancestry, socioeconomic status, and cultural lifestyle: "[Black](/wiki/Black_people "Black people")", "[White](/wiki/White_people "White people")", "[Coloured](/wiki/Coloureds "Coloureds")", and "[Indian](/wiki/Indian_South_Africans "Indian South Africans")", the last two of which included several sub-classifications.[[11]](#cite_note-13) Places of residence were determined by racial classification.[[10]](#cite_note-Routledge-12) Between 1960 and 1983, 3.5 million [black Africans](/wiki/Black_Africans "Black Africans") were removed from their homes and forced into segregated neighbourhoods as a result of apartheid legislation, in some of the largest mass evictions in modern history.[[12]](#cite_note-Forced_Removals-14) Most of these targeted removals were intended to restrict the black population to ten designated "tribal homelands", also known as *[bantustans](/wiki/Bantustan "Bantustan")*, four of which became nominally independent states.[[10]](#cite_note-Routledge-12) The government announced that relocated persons would lose their South African citizenship as they were absorbed into the bantustans.[[9]](#cite_note-Crompton-11)

Apartheid sparked significant international and domestic opposition, resulting in some of the most influential global social movements of the 20th century.[[13]](#cite_note-Lodge-15) It was the target of frequent condemnation in the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") and brought about extensive [international sanctions](/wiki/International_sanctions_during_apartheid "International sanctions during apartheid"), including [arms embargoes](/wiki/Arms_embargo "Arms embargo") and [economic sanctions](/wiki/Economic_sanctions "Economic sanctions") on South Africa.[[14]](#cite_note-Lodge_1983-16) During the 1970s and 1980s, [internal resistance to apartheid](/wiki/Internal_resistance_to_apartheid "Internal resistance to apartheid") became increasingly militant, prompting brutal crackdowns by the [National Party](/wiki/National_Party_(South_Africa) "National Party (South Africa)") ruling government and protracted sectarian violence that left thousands dead or in detention.[[15]](#cite_note-Pandey-17) The [Truth and Reconciliation Commission](/wiki/Truth_and_Reconciliation_Commission_(South_Africa) "Truth and Reconciliation Commission (South Africa)") found that there were 21,000 deaths from political violence, with 7,000 deaths between 1948 and 1989, and 14,000 deaths and 22,000 injuries in the transition period between 1990 and 1994.[[16]](#cite_note-18)[[17]](#cite_note-19) Some reforms of the apartheid system were undertaken, including allowing for [Indian and Coloured political representation in parliament](/wiki/Tricameral_Parliament "Tricameral Parliament"), but these measures failed to appease most activist groups.[[18]](#cite_note-Diplomacy-20)

Between 1987 and 1993, the National Party entered into [bilateral negotiations](/wiki/Negotiations_to_end_apartheid_in_South_Africa "Negotiations to end apartheid in South Africa") with the [African National Congress](/wiki/African_National_Congress "African National Congress") (ANC), the leading anti-apartheid political movement, for ending segregation and introducing majority rule.[[18]](#cite_note-Diplomacy-20)[[19]](#cite_note-21) In 1990, prominent ANC figures, such as [Nelson Mandela](/wiki/Nelson_Mandela "Nelson Mandela"), were released from prison.[[20]](#cite_note-22) Apartheid legislation was repealed on 17 June 1991,[[2]](#cite_note-Abolished-3) leading to [non-racial elections in April 1994](/wiki/1994_South_African_general_election "1994 South African general election").[[21]](#cite_note-Mitchell-23) Since the end of apartheid, elections have been open and competitive.[[22]](#cite_note-:02-24)

## Precursors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Apartheid&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Precursors")]

Main articles: [History of South Africa (1815–1910)](/wiki/History_of_South_Africa_(1815%E2%80%931910) "History of South Africa (1815–1910)") and [History of South Africa (1910–1948)](/wiki/History_of_South_Africa_(1910%E2%80%931948) "History of South Africa (1910–1948)")

*Apartheid* is an [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans")[[23]](#cite_note-25) word meaning "separateness", or "the state of being apart", literally "apart-ness" or [apart](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/apart#English "wikt:apart")-[hood](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-hood#English "wikt:-hood")" (from the Afrikaans suffix *-heid*).[[24]](#cite_note-26)[[25]](#cite_note-27) Its first recorded use was in 1929.[[10]](#cite_note-Routledge-12)

Racial discrimination against Black people in South Africa dates to the beginning of large-scale European colonisation of South Africa with the [Dutch East India Company](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company")'s establishment of a trading post in the [Cape of Good Hope](/wiki/Cape_of_Good_Hope "Cape of Good Hope") in 1652, which eventually expanded into the [Dutch Cape Colony](/wiki/Dutch_Cape_Colony "Dutch Cape Colony"). The company began the [Khoikhoi–Dutch Wars](/wiki/Khoikhoi%E2%80%93Dutch_Wars "Khoikhoi–Dutch Wars") in which it displaced the local [Khoikhoi people](/wiki/Khoikhoi_people "Khoikhoi people"), replaced them with farms worked by [White settlers](/wiki/White_South_Africans "White South Africans"), and imported Black slaves from across the [Dutch Empire](/wiki/Dutch_Empire "Dutch Empire").[[26]](#cite_note-28) In the days of [slavery](/wiki/Slavery_in_South_Africa "Slavery in South Africa"), slaves required passes to travel away from their owners.

In 1797, the *[Landdrost](/wiki/Landdrost#South_Africa "Landdrost")* and [*Heemraden*](/wiki/Heemraad "Heemraad"), local officials, of [Swellendam](/wiki/Swellendam "Swellendam") and [Graaff-Reinet](/wiki/Graaff-Reinet "Graaff-Reinet") extended [pass laws](/wiki/Pass_laws "Pass laws") beyond slaves and decreed that all Khoikhoi (designated as *Hottentots*) moving about the country for any purpose should carry passes.[[27]](#cite_note-Afrikaner_political_thought:_analysis_and_documents-29) This was confirmed by the [British Colonial government](/wiki/British_Cape_Colony "British Cape Colony") in 1809 by the [Hottentot Proclamation](/wiki/Hottentot_Proclamation "Hottentot Proclamation"), which decreed that if a Khoikhoi were to move they would need a pass from their owner or a local official.[[27]](#cite_note-Afrikaner_political_thought:_analysis_and_documents-29) Ordinance No. 49 of 1828 decreed that prospective Black immigrants were to be granted passes for the sole purpose of seeking work.[[27]](#cite_note-Afrikaner_political_thought:_analysis_and_documents-29) These passes were to be issued for Coloureds and Khoikhoi but not for other Africans, who were nonetheless forced to carry passes.

During the [French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_and_Napoleonic_Wars "French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars"), the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") captured and annexed the Dutch Cape Colony.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClarkWorger201612-30) Under the [1806 Cape Articles of Capitulation](/wiki/Battle_of_Blaauwberg#Articles_of_Capitulation "Battle of Blaauwberg") the new British colonial rulers were required to respect previous legislation enacted under [Roman-Dutch law](/wiki/Roman-Dutch_law "Roman-Dutch law"),[[29]](#cite_note-Introduction_to_Roman-Dutch_Law-31) and this led to a separation of the law in South Africa from [English Common Law](/wiki/English_Common_Law "English Common Law") and a high degree of legislative autonomy. The governors and assemblies that governed the legal process in the various colonies of South Africa launched on an independent legislative path different from the rest of the British Empire.

The United Kingdom's [Slavery Abolition Act 1833](/wiki/Slavery_Abolition_Act_1833 "Slavery Abolition Act 1833") provided for the gradual abolition of slavery in most parts of the British Empire. The trade of slaves was made illegal throughout the British Empire by 1837, with Nigeria and Bahrain being the last British territories to abolish slavery.[[30]](#cite_note-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33)[[32]](#cite_note-34)[[33]](#cite_note-35) The act overrode the Cape Articles of Capitulation. To comply with the act, the South African legislation was expanded to include Ordinance 1 in 1835, which effectively changed the status of slaves to [indentured labourers](/wiki/Indentured_servitude "Indentured servitude"). This was followed by Ordinance 3 in 1848, which introduced an indenture system for [Xhosa](/wiki/Xhosa_people "Xhosa people") that was little different from slavery.

The various South African colonies passed legislation throughout the rest of the 19th century to limit the freedom of unskilled workers, to increase the restrictions on indentured workers and to regulate the relations between the races. The discoveries of diamonds and gold in South Africa also raised racial inequality between White people and Black people.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClarkWorger201614-36) Some scholars have argued that Apartheid's ideology can be reflected in [Afrikaner Calvinism](/wiki/Afrikaner_Calvinism "Afrikaner Calvinism"), with its parallel traditions of racialism;[[35]](#cite_note-SaulDubow-37) for example, as early as 1933; the executive council of the Broederbond formulated a recommendation for mass segregation.[[35]](#cite_note-SaulDubow-37)

In the [Cape Colony](/wiki/Cape_Colony "Cape Colony"), which previously had a liberal and multi-racial constitution and a system of [Cape Qualified Franchise](/wiki/Cape_Qualified_Franchise "Cape Qualified Franchise") open to men of all races, the [Franchise and Ballot Act](/wiki/Franchise_and_Ballot_Act "Franchise and Ballot Act") of 1892 raised the [property franchise qualification](/wiki/Property_qualification "Property qualification") and added an educational element, disenfranchising a disproportionate number of the Cape's non-White voters,[[36]](#cite_note-38) and the [Glen Grey Act](/wiki/Glen_Grey_Act "Glen Grey Act") of 1894 instigated by the government of Prime Minister [Cecil Rhodes](/wiki/Cecil_Rhodes "Cecil Rhodes") limited the amount of land Africans could hold. Similarly, in [Natal](/wiki/Colony_of_Natal "Colony of Natal"), the [Natal Legislative Assembly Bill](/wiki/Natal_Legislative_Assembly_Bill "Natal Legislative Assembly Bill") of 1894 deprived Indians of the right to vote.[[37]](#cite_note-39) In 1896 the [South African Republic](/wiki/South_African_Republic "South African Republic") brought in two pass laws requiring Africans to carry a badge. Only those employed by a master were permitted to remain on the [Rand](/wiki/Witwatersrand "Witwatersrand"), and those entering a "labour district" needed a special pass.[[38]](#cite_note-40) During the [Second Boer War](/wiki/Second_Boer_War "Second Boer War"), the British Empire cited racial exploitation of Blacks as a cause for its war against the [Boer republics](/wiki/Boer_republics "Boer republics"). However, the peace negotiations for the [Treaty of Vereeniging](/wiki/Treaty_of_Vereeniging "Treaty of Vereeniging") demanded "the just predominance of the white race" in South Africa as a precondition for the Boer republics unifying with the British Empire.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClarkWorger201616–17-41)

In 1905 the [General Pass Regulations Act](/w/index.php?title=General_Pass_Regulations_Act&action=edit&redlink=1 "General Pass Regulations Act (page does not exist)") denied Black people the vote and limited them to fixed areas,[[40]](#cite_note-42) and in 1906 the [Asiatic Registration Act](/wiki/Asiatic_Registration_Act "Asiatic Registration Act") of the [Transvaal Colony](/wiki/Transvaal_Colony "Transvaal Colony") required all Indians to register and carry passes.[[41]](#cite_note-43) Beginning in 1906 the South African Native Affairs Commission under [Godfrey Lagden](/wiki/Godfrey_Lagden "Godfrey Lagden") began implementing a more openly segregationist policy towards non-Whites.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClarkWorger201617-44) The latter was repealed by the British government but re-enacted in 1908. In 1910, the [Union of South Africa](/wiki/Union_of_South_Africa "Union of South Africa") was created as a self-governing [dominion](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion"), which continued the legislative program: the [South Africa Act](/wiki/South_Africa_Act_1909 "South Africa Act 1909") (1910) enfranchised White people, giving them complete political control over all other racial groups while removing the right of Black people to sit in parliament;[[43]](#cite_note-leach-45) the [Native Land Act](/wiki/Natives_Land_Act,_1913 "Natives Land Act, 1913") (1913) prevented Black people, except those in the Cape, from buying land outside "reserves";[[43]](#cite_note-leach-45) the Natives in Urban Areas Bill (1918) was designed to force Black people into "locations";[[44]](#cite_note-46) the Urban Areas Act (1923) introduced residential segregation and provided cheap labour for industry led by White people; the Colour Bar Act (1926) prevented Black mine workers from practising skilled trades; the [Native Administration Act](/wiki/Native_Administration_Act,_1927 "Native Administration Act, 1927") (1927) made the [British Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") rather than [paramount chiefs](/wiki/Paramount_chief "Paramount chief") the supreme head over all African affairs;[[45]](#cite_note-47)[[46]](#cite_note-48)the Native Land and Trust Act (1936) complemented the 1913 Native Land Act and, in the same year, the [Representation of Natives Act](/wiki/Representation_of_Natives_Act,_1936 "Representation of Natives Act, 1936") removed previous Black voters from the Cape [voters' roll](/wiki/Electoral_roll "Electoral roll") and allowed them to elect three Whites to Parliament.[[47]](#cite_note-49)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

The [United Party](/wiki/United_Party_(South_Africa) "United Party (South Africa)") government of [Jan Smuts](/wiki/Jan_Smuts "Jan Smuts") began to move away from the rigid enforcement of segregationist laws during World War II, but faced growing opposition from [Afrikaner nationalists](/wiki/Afrikaner_nationalism "Afrikaner nationalism") who wanted stricter segregation.[[48]](#cite_note-50)[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClarkWorger201626-51) Post-war, one of the first pieces of segregating legislation enacted by Smuts' government was the [Asiatic Land Tenure Bill (1946)](/wiki/Asiatic_Land_Tenure_and_Indian_Representation_Act,_1946 "Asiatic Land Tenure and Indian Representation Act, 1946"), which banned land sales to Indians and Indian descendant South Africans.[[50]](#cite_note-52) The same year, the government established the [Fagan Commission](/wiki/Fagan_Commission "Fagan Commission"). Amid fears that [integration](/wiki/Racial_integration "Racial integration") would eventually lead to racial assimilation, the Opposition [Herenigde Nasionale Party](/wiki/Herenigde_Nasionale_Party "Herenigde Nasionale Party") (HNP) established the [Sauer Commission](/wiki/Sauer_Commission "Sauer Commission") to investigate the effects of the United Party's policies. The commission concluded that integration would bring about a "loss of personality" for all racial groups. The HNP incorporated the commission's findings into its campaign platform for the [1948 South African general election](/wiki/1948_South_African_general_election "1948 South African general election"), which it won.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Institution and development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Apartheid&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Institution and development")]

Apartheid developed from the racism of colonial factions and due to South Africa's "unique industrialisation".[[51]](#cite_note-NigelWorden-53) The policies of [industrialisation](/wiki/Industrialisation "Industrialisation") led to the segregation and classing of people, which was "specifically developed to nurture early industry such as [mining](/wiki/Mining "Mining")".[[51]](#cite_note-NigelWorden-53) Cheap labour was the basis of the economy and this was taken from what the state classed as peasant groups and the migrants.[[52]](#cite_note-54) To a large extent, the political ideology of apartheid had emerged from the colonisation of Africa by European powers which institutionalised racial discrimination and exercised a paternal philosophy of "[civilising inferior natives](/wiki/Civilizing_mission "Civilizing mission")".[[53]](#cite_note-PaulMaylam-55)

### 1948 election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Apartheid&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1948 election")]

Main article: [South African general election, 1948](/wiki/South_African_general_election,_1948 "South African general election, 1948")

South Africa had allowed social custom and law to govern the consideration of multiracial affairs and of the allocation, in racial terms, of access to economic, social, and political status.[[54]](#cite_note-zastudy-56) Nevertheless, by 1948 it remained apparent that there were gaps in the social structure, whether legislated or otherwise, concerning the rights and opportunities of non-whites. The rapid economic development of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") attracted black migrant workers in large numbers to chief industrial centres, where they compensated for the wartime shortage of white labour. However, this escalated rate of black urbanisation went unrecognised by the South African government, which failed to accommodate the influx with parallel expansion in housing or social services.[[54]](#cite_note-zastudy-56) Overcrowding, increasing crime rates, and disillusionment resulted; urban blacks came to support a new generation of leaders influenced by the principles of [self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination") and popular freedoms enshrined in such statements as the [Atlantic Charter](/wiki/Atlantic_Charter "Atlantic Charter"). Black political organisations and leaders such as [Alfred Xuma](/wiki/Alfred_Xuma "Alfred Xuma"), [James Mpanza](/wiki/James_Mpanza "James Mpanza"), the [African National Congress](/wiki/African_National_Congress "African National Congress"), and the [Council of Non-European Trade Unions](/wiki/Council_of_Non-European_Trade_Unions "Council of Non-European Trade Unions") began demanding political rights, land reform, and the right to unionise.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClarkWorger201637–38-57)

Whites reacted negatively to the changes, allowing the [Herenigde Nasionale Party](/wiki/Herenigde_Nasionale_Party "Herenigde Nasionale Party") (or simply the National Party) to convince a large segment of the [voting bloc](/wiki/Voting_bloc "Voting bloc") that the impotence of the United Party in curtailing the evolving position of nonwhites indicated that the organisation had fallen under the influence of Western liberals.[[54]](#cite_note-zastudy-56) Many [Afrikaners](/wiki/Afrikaners "Afrikaners") resented what they perceived as disempowerment by an underpaid black workforce and the superior economic power and prosperity of white English speakers.[[56]](#cite_note-58) Smuts, as a strong advocate of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), lost domestic support when South Africa was criticised for its [colour bar](/wiki/Racial_segregation "Racial segregation") and the continued [mandate](/wiki/League_of_Nations_mandate "League of Nations mandate") of [South West Africa](/wiki/South_West_Africa "South West Africa") by other UN member states.[[57]](#cite_note-O'Meara-59)

[Afrikaner nationalists](/wiki/Afrikaner_nationalism "Afrikaner nationalism") proclaimed that they offered the voters a new policy to ensure continued white domination.[[58]](#cite_note-Meredith-60) This policy was initially expounded from a theory drafted by [Hendrik Verwoerd](/wiki/Hendrik_Verwoerd "Hendrik Verwoerd") and was presented to the National Party by the [Sauer Commission](/wiki/Sauer_Commission "Sauer Commission").[[54]](#cite_note-zastudy-56) It called for a systematic effort to organise the relations, rights, and privileges of the races as officially defined through a series of parliamentary acts and administrative decrees. Segregation had thus far been pursued only in major matters, such as separate schools, and local society rather than law had been depended upon to enforce most separation; it should now be extended to everything.[[54]](#cite_note-zastudy-56) The commission's goal was to completely remove blacks from areas designated for whites, including cities, with the exception of temporary migrant labour. Blacks would then be encouraged to create their own political units in land reserved for them.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClarkWorger201641–42-61) The party gave this policy a name – *apartheid*. Apartheid was to be the basic ideological and practical foundation of Afrikaner politics for the next quarter of a century.[[58]](#cite_note-Meredith-60)

The National Party's election platform stressed that apartheid would preserve a market for white employment in which non-whites could not compete. On the issues of black urbanisation, the regulation of non-white labour, "influx control", social security, farm tariffs and non-white taxation, the United Party's policy remained contradictory and confused.[[57]](#cite_note-O'Meara-59) Its traditional bases of support not only took mutually exclusive positions, but found themselves increasingly at odds with each other. Smuts' reluctance to consider [South African foreign policy](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_South_Africa "Foreign relations of South Africa") against the mounting tensions of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") also stirred up discontent, while the nationalists promised to purge the state and public service of communist sympathisers.[[57]](#cite_note-O'Meara-59)

First to desert the United Party were Afrikaner farmers, who wished to see a change in influx control due to problems with squatters, as well as higher prices for their maize and other produce in the face of the mineowners' demand for cheap food policies. Always identified with the affluent and capitalist, the party also failed to appeal to its working class constituents.[[57]](#cite_note-O'Meara-59)

Populist rhetoric allowed the National Party to sweep eight constituencies in the mining and industrial centres of the [Witwatersrand](/wiki/Witwatersrand "Witwatersrand") and five more in [Pretoria](/wiki/Pretoria "Pretoria"). Barring the predominantly English-speaking landowner electorate of the [Natal](/wiki/Natal_Province "Natal Province"), the United Party was defeated in almost every rural district. Its urban losses in the nation's most populous province, the [Transvaal](/wiki/Transvaal_Province "Transvaal Province"), proved equally devastating.[[57]](#cite_note-O'Meara-59) As the [voting system](/wiki/Electoral_system "Electoral system") was [disproportionately weighted](/wiki/Malapportionment "Malapportionment") in favour of rural constituencies and the Transvaal in particular, the 1948 election catapulted the Herenigde Nasionale Party from a small minority party to a commanding position with an eight-vote parliamentary lead.[[60]](#cite_note-62)[[61]](#cite_note-sahistory-1948election-63) [Daniel François Malan](/wiki/Daniel_Fran%C3%A7ois_Malan "Daniel François Malan") became the first nationalist prime minister, with the aim of implementing the apartheid philosophy and silencing liberal opposition.[[54]](#cite_note-zastudy-56)

When the National Party came to power in 1948, there were factional differences in the party about the implementation of systemic racial segregation. The "[baasskap](/wiki/Baasskap "Baasskap")" (white domination or supremacist) faction, which was the dominant faction in the NP and state institutions, favoured systematic segregation, but also favoured the participation of black Africans in the economy, with black labour controlled to advance the economic gains of Afrikaners. A second faction were the "purists", who believed in "vertical segregation", in which blacks and whites would be entirely separated, with blacks living in native reserves, with separate political and economic structures, which, they believed, would entail severe short-term pain, but would also lead to independence of white South Africa from black labour in the long term. A third faction, which included [Hendrik Verwoerd](/wiki/Hendrik_Verwoerd "Hendrik Verwoerd"), sympathised with the purists, but allowed for the use of black labour, while implementing the purist goal of vertical separation.[[62]](#cite_note-Kuperus1999-64) Verwoerd would refer to this policy as a policy of "good neighbourliness" as a means of justifying such segregation.[[63]](#cite_note-Indiana_University_Press-65)

Main article: [Apartheid legislation](/wiki/Apartheid_legislation "Apartheid legislation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Zuid_Afrikaanse_premier_dr._H._Verwoerd%2C_Bestanddeelnr_911-1297_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Zuid_Afrikaanse_premier_dr._H._Verwoerd%2C_Bestanddeelnr_911-1297_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zuid_Afrikaanse_premier_dr._H._Verwoerd,_Bestanddeelnr_911-1297_(cropped).jpg)

[Hendrik Verwoerd](/wiki/Hendrik_Verwoerd "Hendrik Verwoerd"), minister of native affairs (1950–1958) and prime minister (1958–1966), earned the nickname 'Architect of Apartheid' from his large role in creating legislation.

NP leaders argued that South Africa did not comprise a single nation, but was made up of four distinct racial groups: white, black, Coloured and Indian. Such groups were split into 13 nations or racial federations. White people encompassed the English and [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans") language groups; the black populace was divided into ten such groups.

The state passed laws that paved the way for "grand apartheid", which was centred on separating races on a large scale, by compelling people to live in separate places defined by race. This strategy was in part adopted from "left-over" British rule that separated different racial groups after they took control of the [Boer republics](/wiki/Boer_republics "Boer republics") in the [Anglo-Boer war](/wiki/Anglo-Boer_war "Anglo-Boer war"). This created the black-only "[townships](/wiki/Township_(South_Africa) "Township (South Africa)")" or "locations", where blacks were relocated to their own towns. As the NP government's [minister of native affairs](/wiki/Minister_of_Bantu_Administration_and_Development,_and_Bantu_Education "Minister of Bantu Administration and Development, and Bantu Education") from 1950, Hendrik Verwoerd had a significant role in crafting such laws, which led to him being regarded as the 'Architect of Apartheid'.[[64]](#cite_note-66)[[63]](#cite_note-Indiana_University_Press-65)[[65]](#cite_note-67) In addition, "petty apartheid" laws were passed.

The first grand apartheid law was the [Population Registration Act](/wiki/Population_Registration_Act "Population Registration Act") of 1950, which formalised racial classification and introduced an identity card for all persons over the age of 18, specifying their racial group.[[66]](#cite_note-68) Official teams or boards were established to come to a conclusion on those people whose race was unclear.[[67]](#cite_note-69) This caused difficulty, especially for [Coloured people](/wiki/Coloureds "Coloureds"), separating families when members were assigned to different races.[[68]](#cite_note-70)

The second pillar of grand apartheid was the [Group Areas Act](/wiki/Group_Areas_Act "Group Areas Act") of 1950.[[69]](#cite_note-71) Until then, most settlements had people of different races living side by side. This Act put an end to diverse areas and determined where one lived according to race. Each race was allotted its own area, which was used in later years as a basis of forced removal.[[70]](#cite_note-72) The [Prevention of Illegal Squatting Act](/wiki/Prevention_of_Illegal_Squatting_Act,_1951 "Prevention of Illegal Squatting Act, 1951") of 1951 allowed the government to demolish black [shanty town](/wiki/Shanty_town "Shanty town") slums and forced white employers to pay for the construction of housing for those black workers who were permitted to reside in cities otherwise reserved for whites.[[71]](#cite_note-73) The [Native Laws Amendment Act, 1952](/wiki/Native_Laws_Amendment_Act,_1952 "Native Laws Amendment Act, 1952") centralised and tightened pass laws so that blacks could not stay in urban areas for longer than 72 hours without a permit.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClarkWorger201649-74)

The [Prohibition of Mixed Marriages Act](/wiki/Prohibition_of_Mixed_Marriages_Act "Prohibition of Mixed Marriages Act") of 1949 prohibited marriage between persons of different races, and the [Immorality Act](/wiki/Immorality_Act "Immorality Act") of 1950 made [sexual relations between whites and other races](/wiki/Miscegenation "Miscegenation") a [criminal offence](/wiki/Criminal_offence "Criminal offence").

Under the [Reservation of Separate Amenities Act](/wiki/Reservation_of_Separate_Amenities_Act "Reservation of Separate Amenities Act") of 1953, municipal grounds could be reserved for a particular race, creating, among other things, separate beaches, buses, hospitals, schools and universities. Signboards such as "whites only" applied to public areas, even including park benches.[[73]](#cite_note-75) Black South Africans were provided with services greatly inferior to those of whites, and, to a lesser extent, to those of Indian and Coloured people.[[74]](#cite_note-crdi-76)

Further laws had the aim of suppressing resistance, especially armed resistance, to apartheid. The [Suppression of Communism Act](/wiki/Suppression_of_Communism_Act "Suppression of Communism Act") of 1950 banned the [Communist Party of South Africa](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_South_Africa "Communist Party of South Africa") and any party subscribing to [Communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism"). The act defined Communism and its aims so sweepingly that anyone who opposed government policy risked being labelled as a Communist. Since the law specifically stated that Communism aimed to disrupt racial harmony, it was frequently used to gag opposition to apartheid. Disorderly gatherings were banned, as were certain organisations that were deemed threatening to the government. It also empowered the Ministry of Justice to impose [banning orders](/wiki/Banning_order "Banning order").[[75]](#cite_note-:1-77)

After the [Defiance Campaign](/wiki/Defiance_Campaign "Defiance Campaign"), the government used the act for the mass arrests and banning of leaders of dissident groups such as the [African National Congress](/wiki/African_National_Congress "African National Congress") (ANC), the [South African Indian Congress](/wiki/South_African_Indian_Congress "South African Indian Congress") (SAIC), and the [South African Congress of Trade Unions](/wiki/South_African_Congress_of_Trade_Unions "South African Congress of Trade Unions") (SACTU). After the release of the Freedom Charter, 156 leaders of these groups were charged in the [1956 Treason Trial](/wiki/1956_Treason_Trial "1956 Treason Trial"). It established [censorship](/wiki/Censorship_in_South_Africa "Censorship in South Africa") of film, literature, and the media under the Customs and Excise Act 1955 and the Official Secrets Act 1956. The same year, the Native Administration Act 1956 allowed the government to banish blacks.[[75]](#cite_note-:1-77)

The [Bantu Authorities Act of 1951](/wiki/Bantu_Authorities_Act,_1951 "Bantu Authorities Act, 1951") created separate government structures for blacks and whites and was the first piece of legislation to support the government's plan of separate development in the [bantustans](/wiki/Bantustan "Bantustan"). The [Bantu Education Act, 1953](/wiki/Bantu_Education_Act,_1953 "Bantu Education Act, 1953") established a separate education system for blacks emphasizing [African culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Africa "Culture of Africa") and [vocational training](/wiki/Vocational_education "Vocational education") under the Ministry of Native Affairs and defunded most [mission schools](/wiki/Mission_school "Mission school").[[76]](#cite_note-78) The [Promotion of Black Self-Government Act](/wiki/Promotion_of_Bantu_Self-government_Act,_1959 "Promotion of Bantu Self-government Act, 1959") of 1959 entrenched the NP policy of nominally independent "homelands" for blacks. So-called "self–governing Bantu units" were proposed, which would have devolved administrative powers, with the promise later of [autonomy](/wiki/Autonomy "Autonomy") and self-government. It also abolished the seats of white representatives of black South Africans and removed from the rolls the few blacks still qualified to vote. The [Bantu Investment Corporation Act](/wiki/Bantu_Investment_Corporation_Act "Bantu Investment Corporation Act") of 1959 set up a mechanism to transfer capital to the homelands to create employment there. Legislation of 1967 allowed the government to stop industrial development in "white" cities and redirect such development to the "homelands". The [Black Homeland Citizenship Act](/wiki/Black_Homeland_Citizenship_Act "Black Homeland Citizenship Act") of 1970 marked a new phase in the Bantustan strategy. It changed the status of blacks to citizens of one of the ten autonomous territories. The aim was to ensure a demographic majority of white people within South Africa by having all ten Bantustans achieve full independence.

Inter-racial contact in sport was frowned upon, but there were no segregatory sports laws.

The government tightened pass laws compelling blacks to carry identity documents, to prevent the immigration of blacks from other countries. To reside in a city, blacks had to be in employment there. Until 1956 women were for the most part excluded from these *pass* requirements, as attempts to introduce *pass laws* for women were met with fierce resistance.[[77]](#cite_note-79)

### Disenfranchisement of Coloured voters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Apartheid&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Disenfranchisement of Coloured voters")]

Main article: [Coloured vote constitutional crisis](/wiki/Coloured_vote_constitutional_crisis "Coloured vote constitutional crisis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Cape-coloured-children.jpg/250px-Cape-coloured-children.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cape-coloured-children.jpg)

Cape Coloured children in [Bonteheuwel](/wiki/Bonteheuwel "Bonteheuwel")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Annual_per_capita_personal_income_by_race_group_in_South_Africa_relative_to_white_levels.svg/250px-Annual_per_capita_personal_income_by_race_group_in_South_Africa_relative_to_white_levels.svg.png)](/w/index.php?title=File:Annual_per_capita_personal_income_by_race_group_in_South_Africa_relative_to_white_levels.svg&lang=en)

Annual per capita personal income by race group in South Africa relative to white levels

In 1950, D. F. Malan announced the NP's intention to create a Coloured Affairs Department.[[78]](#cite_note-80) [J.G. Strijdom](/wiki/J.G._Strijdom "J.G. Strijdom"), Malan's successor as prime minister, moved to strip voting rights from black and Coloured residents of the Cape Province. The previous government had introduced the Separate Representation of Voters Bill into Parliament in 1951, turning it into an [Act](/wiki/Separate_Representation_of_Voters_Act,_1951 "Separate Representation of Voters Act, 1951") on 18 June 1951; however, four voters, G Harris, W D Franklin, W D Collins and Edgar Deane, challenged its validity in court with support from the United Party.[[79]](#cite_note-81) The Cape Supreme Court upheld the act, but was reversed by the Appeal Court, finding the act invalid because a two-thirds majority in a joint sitting of both Houses of [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_South_Africa "Parliament of South Africa") was needed to change the [entrenched clauses](/wiki/Entrenched_clause "Entrenched clause") of the [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_South_Africa#Previous_constitutions_of_South_Africa "Constitution of South Africa").[[80]](#cite_note-82) The government then introduced the High Court of Parliament Bill (1952), which gave Parliament the power to overrule decisions of the court.[[81]](#cite_note-83) The Cape Supreme Court and the Appeal Court declared this invalid too.[[82]](#cite_note-84)

In 1955 the Strijdom government increased the number of judges in the Appeal Court from five to 11, and appointed pro-Nationalist judges to fill the new places.[[83]](#cite_note-85) In the same year they introduced the Senate Act, which increased the Senate from 49 seats to 89.[[84]](#cite_note-86) Adjustments were made such that the NP controlled 77 of these seats.[[85]](#cite_note-87) The parliament met in a joint sitting and passed the [Separate Representation of Voters Act](/wiki/Separate_Representation_of_Voters_Act "Separate Representation of Voters Act") in 1956, which transferred Coloured voters from the common voters' roll in the Cape to a new Coloured voters' roll.[[86]](#cite_note-88) Immediately after the vote, the Senate was restored to its original size. The Senate Act was contested in the Supreme Court, but the recently enlarged Appeal Court, packed with government-supporting judges, upheld the act, and also the Act to remove Coloured voters.[[87]](#cite_note-89)

The 1956 law allowed Coloureds to elect four people to Parliament, but a 1969 law abolished those seats and stripped Coloureds of their right to vote. Since Indians had never been allowed to vote, this resulted in whites being the sole enfranchised group.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Separate representatives for coloured voters were first elected in the [general election of 1958](/wiki/1958_South_African_general_election "1958 South African general election"). Even this limited representation did not last, being ended from 1970 by the [Separate Representation of Voters Amendment Act, 1968](/wiki/Separate_Representation_of_Voters_Amendment_Act,_1968 "Separate Representation of Voters Amendment Act, 1968"). Instead, all coloured adults were given the right to vote for the [Coloured Persons Representative Council](/wiki/Coloured_Persons_Representative_Council "Coloured Persons Representative Council"), which had limited legislative powers. The council was in turn dissolved in 1980. In 1984 a new constitution introduced the [Tricameral Parliament](/wiki/Tricameral_Parliament "Tricameral Parliament") in which coloured voters elected the [House of Representatives](/wiki/House_of_Representatives_of_South_Africa "House of Representatives of South Africa").

A 2016 study in *[The Journal of Politics](/wiki/The_Journal_of_Politics "The Journal of Politics")* suggests that disenfranchisement in South Africa had a significant negative effect on basic service delivery to the disenfranchised.[[88]](#cite_note-90)

### Division among whites

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Apartheid&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Division among whites")]

Before South Africa became a republic in 1961, politics among white South Africans was typified by the division between the mainly [Afrikaner](/wiki/Afrikaner "Afrikaner") pro-republic conservative and the largely English anti-republican liberal sentiments,[[89]](#cite_note-91) with the legacy of the [Boer War](/wiki/Second_Boer_War "Second Boer War") still a factor for some people. Once South Africa became a republic, Prime Minister [Hendrik Verwoerd](/wiki/Hendrik_Verwoerd "Hendrik Verwoerd") called for improved relations and greater accord between people of British descent and the Afrikaners.[[90]](#cite_note-92) He claimed that the only difference was between those in favour of apartheid and those against it. The ethnic division would no longer be between Afrikaans and English speakers, but between blacks and whites.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Most Afrikaners supported the notion of unanimity of white people to ensure their safety. White voters of British descent were divided. Many had opposed a republic, leading to a majority "no" vote in [Natal](/wiki/Natal_Province "Natal Province").[[91]](#cite_note-93) Later, some of them recognised the perceived need for white unity, convinced by the growing trend of decolonisation elsewhere in Africa, which concerned them. British Prime Minister [Harold Macmillan](/wiki/Harold_Macmillan "Harold Macmillan")'s "[Wind of Change](/wiki/Wind_of_Change_(speech) "Wind of Change (speech)")" speech left the British faction feeling that the United Kingdom had abandoned them.[[92]](#cite_note-94) The more conservative English speakers supported Verwoerd;[[93]](#cite_note-95) others were troubled by the severing of ties with the UK and remained loyal to [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown").[[94]](#cite_note-96) They were displeased by having to choose between British and South African nationalities. Although Verwoerd tried to bond these different blocs, the subsequent voting illustrated only a minor swell of support,[[95]](#cite_note-97) indicating that a great many English speakers remained apathetic and that Verwoerd had not succeeded in uniting the white population.

## Homeland system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Apartheid&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Homeland system")]

Main article: [Bantustan](/wiki/Bantustan "Bantustan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/South_Africa_%26_South_West_Africa_Bantustans_Map.svg/500px-South_Africa_%26_South_West_Africa_Bantustans_Map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:South_Africa_%26_South_West_Africa_Bantustans_Map.svg)

Map of the 20 bantustans in South Africa and South West Africa

Under the homeland system, the government attempted to divide South Africa and South West Africa into a number of separate states, each of which was supposed to develop into a separate nation-state for a different ethnic group.[[96]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-98)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Territorial separation was hardly a new institution. There were, for example, the "reserves" created under the British government in the nineteenth century. Under apartheid, 13 percent of the land was reserved for black homelands, a small amount relative to its total population, and generally in economically unproductive areas of the country. The [Tomlinson Commission](/wiki/Tomlinson_Commission "Tomlinson Commission") of 1954 justified apartheid and the homeland system, but stated that additional land ought to be given to the homelands, a recommendation that was not carried out.[[97]](#cite_note-99)

When Verwoerd became prime minister in 1958, the policy of "separate development" came into being, with the homeland structure as one of its cornerstones. Verwoerd came to believe in the granting of independence to these homelands. The government justified its plans on the ostensible basis that the "government's policy is, therefore, not a policy of discrimination on the grounds of race or colour, but a policy of differentiation on the ground of nationhood, of different nations, granting to each self-determination within the borders of their homelands – hence this policy of separate development".[[98]](#cite_note-100) Under the homelands system, blacks would no longer be citizens of South Africa, becoming citizens of the independent homelands who worked in South Africa as foreign migrant labourers on temporary work permits. In 1958 the Promotion of Black Self-Government Act was passed, and border industries and the [Bantu Investment Corporation](/wiki/Bantu_Investment_Corporation_Act,_1959 "Bantu Investment Corporation Act, 1959") were established to promote economic development and the provision of employment in or near the homelands. Many black South Africans who had never resided in their identified homeland were forcibly removed from the cities to the homelands.

The vision of a South Africa divided into multiple [ethnostates](/wiki/Ethnocracy "Ethnocracy") appealed to the reform-minded Afrikaner intelligentsia, and it provided a more coherent philosophical and moral framework for the National Party's policies, while also providing a veneer of intellectual respectability to the controversial policy of so-called [baasskap](/wiki/Baasskap "Baasskap").[[99]](#cite_note-sparksNews24Appalled-101)[[100]](#cite_note-politicsWebRememberingVerwoerd-102)[[101]](#cite_note-afrikanerDominationNews24-103)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Ciskei2.jpg/250px-Ciskei2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ciskei2.jpg)

Rural area in [Ciskei](/wiki/Ciskei "Ciskei"), one of the four nominally independent homelands

In total, 20 homelands were allocated to ethnic groups, ten in South Africa proper and ten in South West Africa. Of these 20 homelands, 19 were classified as black, while one, [Basterland](/wiki/Basterland "Basterland"), was set aside for a sub-group of Coloureds known as [Basters](/wiki/Basters "Basters"), who are closely related to Afrikaners. Four of the homelands were declared independent by the South African government: [Transkei](/wiki/Transkei "Transkei") in 1976, [Bophuthatswana](/wiki/Bophuthatswana "Bophuthatswana") in 1977, [Venda](/wiki/Venda_(Bantustan) "Venda (Bantustan)") in 1979, and [Ciskei](/wiki/Ciskei "Ciskei") in 1981 (known as the TBVC states). Once a homeland was granted its nominal independence, its designated citizens had their South African citizenship revoked and replaced with citizenship in their homeland. These people were then issued passports instead of passbooks. Citizens of the nominally autonomous homelands also had their South African citizenship circumscribed, meaning they were no longer legally considered South African.[[102]](#cite_note-travel-104) The [South African government](/wiki/Government_of_South_Africa "Government of South Africa") attempted to draw an equivalence between their view of black citizens of the homelands and the problems which other countries faced through entry of illegal immigrants.

### International recognition of the Bantustans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Apartheid&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: International recognition of the Bantustans")]

Bantustans within the borders of South Africa and South West Africa were classified by degree of nominal self-rule: 6 were "non-self-governing", 10 were "self-governing", and 4 were "independent". In theory, self-governing Bantustans had control over many aspects of their internal functioning but were not yet sovereign nations. Independent Bantustans (Transkei, Bophutatswana, Venda and Ciskei; also known as the TBVC states) were intended to be fully sovereign. In reality, they had no significant economic infrastructure and with few exceptions encompassed swaths of disconnected territory. This meant all the Bantustans were little more than puppet states controlled by South Africa.

Throughout the existence of the independent Bantustans, South Africa remained the only country to recognise their independence. Nevertheless, internal organisations of many countries, as well as the South African government, lobbied for their recognition. For example, upon the foundation of Transkei, the Swiss-South African Association encouraged the Swiss government to recognise the new state. In 1976, leading up to a United States House of Representatives resolution urging the President to not recognise Transkei, the South African government intensely lobbied lawmakers to oppose the bill.[[103]](#cite_note-105) Each TBVC state extended recognition to the other independent Bantustans while South Africa showed its commitment to the notion of TBVC sovereignty by building embassies in the TBVC capitals.

### Forced removals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Apartheid&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Forced removals")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Travelling_Light_76_F.jpg/250px-Travelling_Light_76_F.jpg)](/wiki/File:Travelling_Light_76_F.jpg)

Man subject to forced removal in Mogopa, Western Transvaal, February 1984

See also: [Group Areas Act](/wiki/Group_Areas_Act "Group Areas Act") and [Abolition of Racially Based Land Measures Act, 1991](/wiki/Abolition_of_Racially_Based_Land_Measures_Act,_1991 "Abolition of Racially Based Land Measures Act, 1991")

During the 1950s, 1960s, 1970s and 1980s, the government implemented a policy of "resettlement", to force people to move to their designated "group areas". Millions of people were forced to relocate. These removals included people relocated due to [slum clearance](/wiki/Slum_clearance_in_South_Africa "Slum clearance in South Africa") programmes, labour tenants on white-owned farms, the inhabitants of the so-called "black spots" (black-owned land surrounded by white farms), the families of workers living in townships close to the homelands, and "surplus people" from urban areas, including thousands of people from the Western Cape (which was declared a "Coloured Labour Preference Area")[[104]](#cite_note-clp-106) who were moved to the [Transkei](/wiki/Transkei "Transkei") and [Ciskei](/wiki/Ciskei "Ciskei") homelands. The best-publicised [forced removals](/wiki/Population_transfer "Population transfer") of the 1950s occurred in [Johannesburg](/wiki/Johannesburg "Johannesburg"), when 60,000 people were moved to the new township of [Soweto](/wiki/Soweto "Soweto") (an abbreviation for South Western Townships).[[105]](#cite_note-107)[[106]](#cite_note-timeSophia-108)

Until 1955, [Sophiatown](/wiki/Sophiatown "Sophiatown") had been one of the few urban areas where black people were allowed to own land, and was slowly developing into a multiracial slum. As industry in Johannesburg grew, Sophiatown became the home of a rapidly expanding black workforce, as it was convenient and close to town. It had the only swimming pool for black children in Johannesburg.[[107]](#cite_note-Meredith2010-109) As one of the oldest black settlements in Johannesburg, it held an almost symbolic importance for the 50,000 black people it contained. Despite a vigorous ANC protest campaign and worldwide publicity, the removal of Sophiatown began on 9 February 1955 under the Western Areas Removal Scheme. In the early hours, heavily armed police forced residents out of their homes and loaded their belongings onto government trucks. The residents were taken to a large tract of land 19 kilometres (12 mi) from the city centre, known as [Meadowlands](/wiki/Meadowlands,_Gauteng "Meadowlands, Gauteng"), which the government had purchased in 1953. Meadowlands became part of a new planned black city called [Soweto](/wiki/Soweto "Soweto"). Sophiatown was destroyed by bulldozers, and a new white suburb named [Triomf](/wiki/Triomf "Triomf") (Triumph) was built in its place. This pattern of forced removal and destruction was to repeat itself over the next few years, and was not limited to black South Africans alone. Forced removals from areas like [Cato Manor](/wiki/Cato_Manor "Cato Manor") (Mkhumbane) in [Durban](/wiki/Durban "Durban"), and [District Six](/wiki/District_Six "District Six") in [Cape Town](/wiki/Cape_Town "Cape Town"), where 55,000 Coloured and [Indian people](/wiki/Indian_South_Africans "Indian South Africans") were forced to move to new townships on the [Cape Flats](/wiki/Cape_Flats "Cape Flats"), were carried out under the [Group Areas Act](/wiki/Group_Areas_Act "Group Areas Act") of 1950. Nearly 600,000 Coloured, Indian and [Chinese people](/wiki/Chinese_South_Africans "Chinese South Africans") were moved under the Group Areas Act. Some 40,000 whites were also forced to move when land was transferred from "white South Africa" into the black homelands.[[108]](#cite_note-110) In South-West Africa, the apartheid plan that instituted Bantustans was as a result of the so-called Odendaal Plan, a set of proposals from the Odendaal Commission of 1962–1964.[[109]](#cite_note-111)
